questions.question: What does Spring Boot's layered JAR feature deliver in a Docker build?
choices: It compresses the JAR for smaller storage.

It splits dependencies, loader, snapshot deps, and application code into separate directories so each maps to its own Docker layer - meaning code-only changes don't invalidate the heavy dependencies layer in the image cache.
It bundles a custom JRE alongside the application classes.
questions.explanation: Layered JARs (default since Spring Boot 2.3) order content from cold to hot: dependencies (rarely change), Spring Boot loader, snapshot deps, application code. A Dockerfile that COPY's each into its own layer benefits from the cache when only the small application layer changes.

questions.question: You set Kubernetes memory limit to 1Gi for your Spring Boot pod and want the JVM to size its heap proportionally rather than hard-coding -Xmx. Which flag?
choices: -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 - the JVM detects the cgroup limit (Java 10+) and sizes the heap as a percentage.
questions.explanation: -XX:MaxRAMPercentage caps heap as a percentage of the available container memory. Combined with the JVM's automatic cgroup detection, this keeps the same image safe across different pod sizes. 75% is a common starting point - the remaining 25% covers native memory, metaspace, threads, and runtime overhead.

questions.question: When is GraalVM Native Image worth the trade-offs (longer build times, reflection caveats) for a Spring Boot service?
choices: Always - native is faster than the JVM in every scenario.
For long-running services with heavy steady-state throughput.
For short-lived workloads where sub-second startup and low memory matter most: serverless functions, CLIs, scale-to-zero containers.
Only when Java 21 virtual threads aren't available.
questions.explanation: Native Image's wins are startup time and memory; its costs are build time, reflection metadata, and slightly worse peak throughput. For long-running JVM workloads the JIT-optimised steady state usually beats native; for serverless / scale-to-zero scenarios native dominates because cold start matters.
